awd · Gotchas

Strong Typing and Variable Hygiene

← Back to Course Package
⚠️ The 'any' Trap
Using the 'any' type bypasses the compiler's safety checks and allows logically broken code to compile without errors. If you use it, you risk runtime crashes and point deductions because you have effectively disabled TypeScript's core purpose.
⚠️ The 'var' Leak
Unlike 'let' or 'const', the 'var' keyword is function-scoped rather than block-scoped, allowing variables to 'leak' out of loops or if-statements. This leads to unintentional side effects where variables remain accessible and mutable in parts of the code where they should have expired.
⚠️ Local 'tsc' Execution
Running the command 'tsc' directly in your terminal will often fail or use the wrong version if TypeScript is installed as a local development dependency. You must prefix the command with 'npx tsc' to ensure the shell locates the binary inside your project's node_modules folder.
⚠️ Interface Case Sensitivity
TypeScript interfaces require an exact character-for-character match, meaning 'id' and 'ID' are treated as completely different properties. Misspelling a property name by even one casing difference will cause the compiler to claim the object is missing required properties, even if the data is present.
